elements.sent-2411-001: SRAM-based Analog Compute-in-Memory (ACiM) demonstrates promising energy efficiency for deep neural network (DNN) processing.
elements.sent-2411-002: Although recent aggressive design strategies have led to successive improvements on efficiency, there is limited discussion regarding the accompanying inference accuracy challenges.
elements.sent-2411-003: Given the growing difficulty in validating ACiM circuits with full-scale DNNs, standardized modeling methodology and open-source inference simulator are urgently needed.
elements.sent-2411-004: This paper presents ASiM, a simulation framework specifically designed to assess inference quality, enabling comparisons of ACiM prototype chips and guiding design decisions.
elements.sent-2411-005: ASiM works as a plug-and-play tool that integrates seamlessly with the PyTorch ecosystem, offering speed and ease of use.
elements.sent-2411-006: Using ASiM, we conducted a comprehensive analysis of how various design factors impact DNN inference.
elements.sent-2411-007: We observed that activation encoding can tolerate certain levels of quantization noise, indicating a substantial potential for bit-parallel scheme to enhance energy efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-008: However, inference accuracy is susceptible to noise, as ACiM circuits typically use limited ADC dynamic range, making even small errors down to 1 LSB significantly deteriorates accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-009: This underscores the need for high design standards, especially for complex DNN models and challenging tasks.
elements.sent-2411-010: In response to these findings, we propose two solutions: Hybrid Compute-in-Memory architecture and majority voting to secure accurate computation of MSB cycles.
elements.sent-2411-011: These approaches improve inference quality while maintaining ACiM’s energy efficiency benefits, offering promising pathways toward reliable ACiM deployment in real-world applications.
elements.sent-2411-013: Recent advances have shown a scaling law in deep learning models, where performance consistently improves as the size of the model increases~\cite{LLM_Scaling .}
elements.sent-2411-014: However, executing these large-scale models in traditional von Neumann architectures leads to substantial energy consumption and latency~\cite{Energy_Problem , which pose critical challenges, particularly for edge devices with limited resources.}
elements.sent-2411-015: As a solution, Compute-in-Memory (CiM) has emerged as a novel paradigm aimed at overcoming these limitations by performing computations directly within the memory where data are stored~\cite{IMC_Advances_Prospects .}
elements.sent-2411-016: This approach contrasts with conventional architectures that separate computation and memory units, CiM integrates computational capabilities into the memory itself, reducing data movement and thus substantially lowering energy consumption and latency.
elements.sent-2411-017: Among the various CiM architectures, Analog CiM (ACiM) emerges as a promising approach to accelerate deep neural network (DNN) computations.
elements.sent-2411-018: Unlike Digital CiM (DCiM), which integrates digital logic within memory arrays, ACiM leverages the intrinsic analog characteristics of memory cells to perform multiply-and-accumulate (MAC) operations in the analog domain~\cite{Analog_or_Digital .}
elements.sent-2411-019: This approach inherently offers higher computational density and energy efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-020: Notably, SRAM-based ACiM has garnered significant attention due to its compatibility with existing CMOS processes.
elements.sent-2411-021: By incorporating capacitors into the SRAM array, the charge-domain SRAM-based CiM achieves high reliability while maintaining impressive energy efficiency~\cite{64-Tile , inspiring researchers to further push the limits of its performance.}
elements.sent-2411-022: In recent years, state-of-the-art ACiM designs have tended to compress input activations and perform MAC operations in a bit-parallel fashion while lowering ADC bit precision to achieve improved energy efficiency~\cite{MACC-SRAM,In-SRAM-ADC_ACIM,PIMCA,One-Shot_AD_MAC,PICO-RAM .}
elements.sent-2411-023: Despite of successive improvement on energy efficiency of ACiM designs, discussion regarding inference accuracy remains limited.
elements.sent-2411-024: This gap of understanding inference accuracy is particularly concerning by the challenges of validating ACiM circuits, because the massive scale of modern DNNs makes full hardware validation increasingly difficult.
elements.sent-2411-025: A more practical approach is partial validation, where researchers measure comprehensive characteristics of ACiM circuits and integrate them into software simulations to evaluate the end-to-end inference accuracy~\cite{Programmable_Accelerator_ISSCC, C3SRAM,MACC-SRAM,In-SRAM-ADC_ACIM .}
elements.sent-2411-026: Although this approach theoretically yields simulation results comparable to full hardware measurements, modeling ACiM circuits presents numerous latent challenges that can lead to misleading accuracy assessments.
elements.sent-2411-027: Moreover, the absence of standardized open-source ACiM simulators hampers reproducing circuit performance and making meaningful comparison across various research groups.
elements.sent-2411-028: In this paper, we introduce ASiM, an open-source simulation framework for designers to evaluate and compare the inference performance of their ACiM circuits, serving as a tool to guide design decisions and advance ACiM development.
elements.sent-2411-029: ASiM provides a comprehensive simulation environment that strictly follows SRAM-based ACiM computing rules while incorporating a variety of design factors to assess their effects on DNN inference.
elements.sent-2411-030: Unlike other frameworks, ASiM is a plug-and-play tool that integrates seamlessly into the widely used PyTorch ecosystem, offering speed and ease of use.
elements.sent-2411-031: Moreover, ASiM supports recent design innovations, such as bit-parallel operation, and extends its functionality to attention-based networks like Transformers.
elements.sent-2411-032: Through ASiM, we performed an in-depth analysis and numerical characterization of how various design factors affect inference accuracy in ACiM circuits.
elements.sent-2411-033: The main findings are as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-034: Noise effects of ACiM circuits can severely impact inference accuracy due to the limited ADC dynamic range utilization in practical applications.
elements.sent-2411-035: Our analysis reveals that even minor ADC readout errors of 1 LSB can propagate through the network and cause significant accuracy degradation.
elements.sent-2411-036: Inference on more complex tasks, such as ImageNet, is inherently more challenging than on CIFAR-10, requiring higher design standards or adoption of more reliable Hybrid CiM (HCiM) approach.
elements.sent-2411-037: While bit-parallel ACiM can tolerate some quantization noise and thus significantly boost ACiM energy efficiency, it is more sensitive to analog noise, necessitating stricter design standards for ACiM circuits.
elements.sent-2411-038: This paper makes the following contributions:.
elements.sent-2411-039: We developed ASiM, an open-source framework for systematic ACiM accuracy evaluation that integrates with PyTorch and supports modern DNN architectures including Transformers.
elements.sent-2411-040: With ASiM, we investigated the impact of key design factors on inference accuracy, and analyze the mechanism of how these factors degrade inference, revealing noise sensitivity issues that are usually overlooked before.
elements.sent-2411-041: Building on our findings, we propose practical design guidelines and solutions to improve ACiM inference accuracy, enhancing its practicality for real-world applications.
elements.sent-2411-042: The remainder of this paper is organized as follows.
elements.sent-2411-043: Section \ref{Chapt_Preliminaries reviews related works and provides preliminary concepts on ACiM and inference evaluation metrics.}
elements.sent-2411-044: Section \ref{Chapt_Framework describes the detailed implementation and modeling methodology of the ASiM framework.}
elements.sent-2411-045: Section \ref{Chapt_Analysis presents a comprehensive analysis of how each ACiM design factor impacts DNN inference accuracy and highlights fundamental limitations.}
elements.sent-2411-046: Section \ref{Chapt_Accurate_Inference proposes simple yet effective solutions to improve ACiM inference accuracy.}
elements.sent-2411-048: CiM accelerators break down multi-bit matrix multiplications into bit-wise MAC operations~\cite{Bit-Flexible_CIM , as described in Eq. \ref{Eq_MAC : \begin{equation}O = \mathbf{x} \cdot \mathbf{w} = \sum_{p = 0}^{P - 1} \sum_{q = 0}^{Q - 1} (-1)^{k} {2}^{p + q} \sum_{n = 0}^{N - 1} x_{n} w_{n}[q] \label{Eq_MAC}\end{equation} Here, $\mathbf{x}$ and $\mathbf{w}$ denote the N-dimensional input activation vector and weight vector, respectively, both represented in integer format with bit widths of P and Q.}}
elements.sent-2411-049: The factor $k \in \{0, 1\}$ is used to manage negative conditions in 2's complement for each bit-wise MAC cycle.
elements.sent-2411-050: In SRAM-based charge domain ACiM, weight bits are stored directly in memory cells to reduce frequent memory accesses, while activations are continuously refreshed and broadcast across the ACiM array.
elements.sent-2411-051: The resulting dot products (DPs) are temporarily stored in local capacitors; the accumulated charge is then shared or coupled along a compute bit-line (CBL), and the voltage is finally read out using ADCs.
elements.sent-2411-052: Early version of bit-serial ACiM operates by broadcasting activations bit by bit, causing local capacitors to undergo either a full charge or discharge during each cycle.
elements.sent-2411-053: This effectively decomposes a matrix multiplication of P and Q bits into $P \times Q$ binary MAC cycles, as shown in Fig. \ref{Fig_ACiM (a).}
elements.sent-2411-054: Progressing from bit-serial ACiM, the bit-parallel ACiM encodes input activations into y-bit compression via DACs, which allows multi-step DP voltage levels in the local capacitors and reduces the MAC cycles approximately to $P / y \times Q$~\cite{PICO-RAM , as illustrated in Fig. \ref{Fig_ACiM (b).}}
elements.sent-2411-055: By minimizing the number of MAC cycles, bit-parallel ACiM significantly enhances energy efficiency.

(a) Bit-serial ACiM. (b) Bit-parallel ACiM.} \label{Fig_ACiM}
elements.sent-2411-056: However, compressing more activation bits into a single ADC readout can potentially degrade inference accuracy, as it introduces higher quantization noise than the traditional bit-serial scheme.
elements.sent-2411-057: Additionally, recent designs often reduce ADC precision to achieve maximum energy efficiency, further amplifying quantization noise, which may compromise DNN inference.
elements.sent-2411-058: Under these conditions, the intrinsic non-idealities of analog computations in ACiM, such as noise and signal distortion, become increasingly problematic.
elements.sent-2411-059: This creates additional complexities in determining optimal design policies.
elements.sent-2411-060: Despite successive advancements in ACiM architectures, there remains a lack of comprehensive analysis on how these factors affect inference accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-061: To fill this critical gap, we have developed ASiM, a comprehensive simulation framework, to analyze these effects and highlight the bottlenecks in charge domain ACiM, providing insights for future ACiM design improvements.
elements.sent-2411-062: Validating a full DNN inference on a prototype ACiM chip is a challenging task that demands substantial effort.
elements.sent-2411-063: As a result, numerous simulation frameworks have been developed to model ACiM performance across various memory types.
elements.sent-2411-064: These frameworks not only evaluate the performance of fabricated chips but also assist in guiding chip specification decisions before ACiM circuit design begins.
elements.sent-2411-065: Some frameworks focus on circuit-level performance optimization, enabling design space exploration~\cite{NeuroSim,SySCIM,PIMSIM-NN , while others emphasize incorporating non-idealities to assess inference accuracy~\cite{aihwkit,MemTorch,eF2lowSim,Fast_Accurate_Sim .}}
elements.sent-2411-066: There are also frameworks that employ unique techniques to address both objectives simultaneously~\cite{X-PIM .}
elements.sent-2411-067: However, most existing frameworks are tailored for non-volatile memories like RRAM and Flash, which operate under different mechanisms and noise characteristics compared to SRAM.
elements.sent-2411-068: Saikia~\cite{Modeling_SRAM_IMC introduces a modeling approach for inference accuracy in charge-domain SRAM ACiM.}
elements.sent-2411-069: However, this model is restricted to a fixed configuration and supports only the ResNet model, limiting its applicability to modern ACiM designs and diverse DNN architectures.
elements.sent-2411-070: Furthermore, the approach uses a specialized setting that clips the ADC quantization range to a narrow range, which is challenging to implement in actual ADCs.
elements.sent-2411-071: As a result, the simulation outputs tend to overestimate inference accuracy, offering limited insights for ACiM design improvements.
elements.sent-2411-072: In this work, we present a user-friendly simulation framework based on PyTorch, designed to be both general and practical for ACiM designers.
elements.sent-2411-073: Unlike most simulators created for RRAM, where each crossbar weight is modeled as a multi-bit value~\cite{Fast_Accurate_Sim,Noisy_Machines , our ASiM framework aligns with SRAM CiM principles by decomposing computations into binary cycles and analyzing the influence of various design parameters on these MAC values, ensuring compatibility with fabricated circuits.}
elements.sent-2411-074: Additionally, ASiM integrates recent advancements in ACiM architecture and machine learning, including bit-parallel operations and support for Transformer models.
elements.sent-2411-075: This enhances its adaptability to a wide range of ACiM hardware configurations and enables detailed performance analysis across different models and tasks.
elements.sent-2411-076: To quantitatively assess the accuracy loss when using an ACiM accelerator for DNN inference, rigorous theoretical analysis and evaluation metrics have been established to guide the circuit design~\cite{Accuracy_Efficiency_Trade_Off .}
elements.sent-2411-077: Among these metrics, the signal-to-quantization noise ratio (SQNR) and compute-signal-to-noise ratio (CSNR) are commonly used to characterize the inference quality of an ACiM chip.
elements.sent-2411-078: SQNR measures the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) by considering only quantization effects during ACiM processing, while CSNR extends this by factoring in all non-idealities introduced by analog computations.
elements.sent-2411-079: The original definitions of SQNR and CSNR are given in~\cite{CSNR_Metrics as: \begin{equation}SQNR = \frac{{\sigma}^{2}_{y}}{{\sigma}^{2}_{y_i}} + \frac{{\sigma}^{2}_{y}}{{\sigma}^{2}_{y_o}} \label{Eq_SQNR_Def}\end{equation} \begin{equation}CSNR = \frac{{\sigma}^{2}_{y}}{{\sigma}^{2}_{y_i}} + \frac{{\sigma}^{2}_{y}}{{\sigma}^{2}_{y_o}} + \frac{{\sigma}^{2}_{y}}{{\sigma}^{2}_{\eta}} \label{Eq_CSNR_Def}\end{equation} where ${\sigma ^{2 _{y}$ is the variance of the ideal floating-point output, and ${\sigma ^{2 _{y_i}$ and ${\sigma ^{2 _{y_o}$ represent the variance of the output considering input and output quantization, respectively.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-080: For CSNR, the additional term ${\sigma ^{2 _{\eta}$ accounts for variations caused by analog non-idealities.}}
elements.sent-2411-081: Jia~\cite{Programmable_BSBP used randomly generated weight and activation vectors, comparing the post-quantization output to the ideal full-precision output to measure the SQNR of a real device, as described by: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} CSNR (SQNR) = \frac{P_{Signal}}{P_{Noise}} = 10 \log_{10} [\frac{\sum^{N-1}_{n=0} y^{2}_{n}}{\sum^{N-1}_{n=0} {(y_{n} - \hat{y_{n}})}^{2}}] \end{aligned} \label{Eq_CSNR_Measure}\end{equation} Here, $y_n$ is the ideal full-precision output, and $\hat{y_n}$ is the expected output from a real device.}
elements.sent-2411-082: Yoshioka~\cite{CR_ACIM_JSSC extended this approach by introducing analog Gaussian noise into each binary MAC cycle to estimate the actual CSNR of ACiM chips.}
elements.sent-2411-083: Although these results suggest a strong CSNR despite the presence of analog noise, our comprehensive investigation indicates that these evaluations may be overly optimistic.
elements.sent-2411-084: This issue stems from assumption that the vectors in the CSNR calculation are uniformly distributed, which does not align with the actual distributions found in DNN applications.
elements.sent-2411-085: While the noise terms in the denominator of Eqs.
elements.sent-2411-086: Assuming a uniform distribution across the ADC’s full dynamic range can result in amplified signal variance, leading to an overestimation of CSNR.
elements.sent-2411-087: In this paper, we demonstrate that the output distribution in ACiM depends on factors such as the DNN model, input activations, and MAC cycle index, with the real output signal typically confined to a smaller range.
elements.sent-2411-088: As a result, the CSNR metric may not effectively capture inference accuracy in real DNN applications.
elements.sent-2411-089: Our ASiM framework, therefore, serves as a more accurate tool for evaluating inference performance in practical DNN tasks and supports design space exploration for ACiM chip design.
elements.sent-2411-090: ASiM is built on PyTorch, the leading deep learning ecosystem, to ensure seamless integration and maximize user convenience.
elements.sent-2411-091: A general overview of ASiM is shown in Fig. \ref{Fig_ASiM .}
elements.sent-2411-092: ASiM is a plug-and-play framework developed for simulating SRAM-based charge domain ACiM.
elements.sent-2411-093: For computations dominated by MACs, such as CONV, Linear, and Attention layers, users can substitute the default PyTorch modules with ASiM modules, providing additional circuit-specific parameters for bit-wise simulations that follow ACiM computation process.
elements.sent-2411-094: During the forward pass in ASiM, quantized weights and activations are decomposed into binary bit tensors, split according to the predefined macro dimensions, and mapped onto the ACiM macros.
elements.sent-2411-095: The bit-wise simulation transforms the original floating-point operations into $wbit \times xbit \times mapping$ times PyTorch functions operating on binary tensors.
elements.sent-2411-096: This method produces ideal voltage values on each CBL column, allowing accurate modeling of analog noise and ADC rounding effect on these values.
elements.sent-2411-097: Importantly, ASiM leverages native PyTorch operations, which allows users to take advantage of GPU acceleration with CUDA, making ASiM both fast and accurate for inference simulations.
elements.sent-2411-098: Additionally, ASiM supports bit-parallel operation, where input activations are encoded based on encoding bit width given by users, and the framework calculates bit-parallel voltage values using pre-configured algorithms, ensuring compatibility with most latest ACiM designs.
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{Figure/Fig_ASiM.pdf} \caption{ASiM framework overview: ASiM is a plug-and-play simulation framework tailored for charge-domain ACiM inference. Users can seamlessly replace default PyTorch modules with ASiM modules to perform inference simulations by providing additional circuit parameters. The framework automatically decomposes the weights/activations and maps it to the designated macros, following ACiM computing rules while incorporating noise and ADC effects.} \label{Fig_ASiM}
elements.sent-2411-099: A sample bit-serial simulation algorithm using the ASiM module is shown in Algorithm \ref{Algorithm_ASiM .}
elements.sent-2411-100: The ASiM module initializes user-provided arguments that define the characteristics of the prototype ACiM circuits.
elements.sent-2411-101: Once initialized, the module quantizes all model weights and input activations, decomposes them into binary bit tensors, and splits these tensors according to the predefined macro dimensions.
elements.sent-2411-102: During the forward pass of the ASiM modules, the ideal CBL voltage for each column is computed by applying PyTorch functions such as conv2d, linear, or matmul on each bit-serial cycle with binary tensors.
elements.sent-2411-103: After determining the ideal voltage value for each cycle, analog noise—based on the predefined noise model—is added to these voltage values.
elements.sent-2411-104: The CBL voltage is subsequently read out by ADCs, which round these analog values to the nearest discrete level based on ADC precision, followed by bit-shifting and accumulation to yield the final output activation values, as described in Eq. \ref{Eq_MAC .}
elements.algorithm-2411-001: \caption{Bit-wise Simulation of ASiM Modules} \label{Algorithm_ASiM} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \REQUIRE Input activations \ENSURE Output activations \STATE Initialize ASiM module with the following parameters: bit width for weights and activations, ADC precision, row parallelism, analog noise intensity, and others \STATE Quantize input activations and weights to $X$ and $W$ bits. Convert them into binary bit tensors and split them into $N$ tensors to match the dimensions of the macros \FOR{Each mapping update: $n = 0, 1, \dots, N-1$} \FOR{Each weight bit index: $i = 0, 1, \dots, W-1$} \FOR{Each input bit index: $j = 0, 1, \dots, X-1$} \STATE Perform bit-wise computation: $V_{MAC} = $nn.functional$(w_i, x_j)$ \IF{Analog noise is included} \STATE Generate and add noise: $V_{MAC} = V_{MAC} + f_{N}(\sigma)$ \ENDIF \STATE Apply ADC rounding function: $MAC_{i,j} = f_{ADC}(V_{MAC})$ \STATE Update multi-bit output activations: $Output = Output + Shift(MAC_{i,j}, i+j)$ \ENDFOR \ENDFOR \ENDFOR \RETURN Output activations computed by the ACiM circuits \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-105: To streamline implementation, per-tensor uniform quantization is employed as the quantization strategy for all model weights and activations in the ASiM modules.
elements.sent-2411-106: For post-ReLU activations in CNNs and post-softmax attention in ViTs, where the activation values are consistently positive, unsigned quantization is used.
elements.sent-2411-107: For all model weights and the remaining activations, signed quantization in 2's complement format is applied, where the MSB represents the sign.
elements.sent-2411-108: In the ASiM configuration, we adopt the weight stationary data flow commonly used in most ACiM designs, where model weights are stored in SRAM cells, and input activations are frequently refreshed and broadcast across the macro.
elements.sent-2411-109: In the MultiheadAttention layers, which involve matrix multiplications (QK and AV), Queries (Q) and Values (V) are stored in SRAM cells, while Keys (K) and Attention Scores (A) are broadcast across the macro, providing an optimal configuration suitable for bit-parallel simulations.
elements.sent-2411-110: In bit-parallel simulations, activations are converted into multi-step voltage values using the specified encoding bit width.
elements.sent-2411-111: For unsigned activations, all bits are encoded directly according to the given parameter.
elements.sent-2411-112: If the activation bit width does not evenly divide the encoding bit width, the encoding begins with the LSB, and the remaining MSB bits are encoded separately.
elements.sent-2411-113: For signed activations, the MSB (sign bit) is processed in a bit-serial manner, while the remaining bits follow the same encoding rules as unsigned activations.
elements.sent-2411-114: As detailed in Algorithm \ref{Algorithm_ASiM , analog noise can be added to the CBL voltage of each column to emulate real ACiM devices, enabling evaluation of its effect on inference accuracy.}
elements.sent-2411-115: ASiM includes two noise models: random noise and non-linearity, both of which can be configured with user-defined noise intensities.
elements.sent-2411-116: The combined analog noise model, incorporating these two noise types, reflects the majority of conditions found in fabricated circuits.
elements.sent-2411-117: Static offset noise, while potentially present in real circuits, can be mitigated through ADC code calibration and is therefore omitted from ASiM for simplicity.
elements.sent-2411-118: Random noise covers sources such as $kT/C$ noise and random comparator decision errors, which can be simply represented with a Gaussian distribution modeling an ADC input-referred noise~\cite{Noise_Modeling_SAR,Random_Decision_Error .}
elements.sent-2411-119: The distribution has a mean of zero and a standard deviation corresponding to the noise intensity, with ASiM taking this intensity as a percentage relative to the entire operational voltage range.
elements.sent-2411-120: Non-linearity arises from device mismatches and layout-specific issues; ASiM incorporates capacitance mismatch in local memory cells and DACs, scaled by $\sqrt{N}$, where $N$ is the number of capacitors in the CBL and DAC~\cite{Cap_Non_linear .}
elements.sent-2411-121: Users can also incorporate custom noise models to account for non-linearities specific to their designs, enabling tailored inference performance analysis with ASiM.
elements.sent-2411-122: ADCs convert the CBL voltage across the entire operational range into discrete steps, determined by ADC precision, as shown by: \begin{equation}MAC = \text{clamp} \left( \left\lfloor V_{MAC} \right\rceil, 0, 2^k - 1 \right) \label{Eq_ADC}\end{equation} where $V_{MAC}$ denotes the CBL voltage before rounding, and $MAC$ represents the digital MAC value after ADC rounding.
elements.sent-2411-123: Here, $k$ is the ADC precision, and $\left\lfloor \cdot \right\rceil$ denotes the round-to-nearest operator.
elements.sent-2411-124: After rounding, the digital output is scaled back to its corresponding MAC value, followed by bit-shifting and accumulation to produce the final multi-bit output activations.
elements.sent-2411-125: In~\cite{Modeling_SRAM_IMC , the ADC’s dynamic range is clipped to a narrow span to increase granularity.}
elements.sent-2411-126: While this approach can theoretically reduce quantization noise, it presents two significant drawbacks: (1) analog noise becomes proportionally more significant within the narrowed range, and (2) the implementation requires complex dynamic range adjustment circuits.
elements.sent-2411-127: Thus, this method tends to degrade the ADC's effective number of bits (ENOB) in practice, potentially leading to optimistic accuracy estimations.
elements.sent-2411-128: In contrast, our ADC model captures the full range of possible CBL voltages, better reflecting real-world ACiM implementations, providing more reliable accuracy predictions while maintaining implementation simplicity.
elements.sent-2411-129: Although ASiM is designed as a plug-and-play framework, directly using the default floating-point model for ACiM inference often leads to significant accuracy loss.
elements.sent-2411-130: To address this, we provide interfaces for quantization-aware training (QAT) and noise-aware training (NAT) for model fine-tuning~\cite{White_Paper_Quantization,Low-Cost_7T .}
elements.sent-2411-131: When set to Train mode and configured with the desired quantization bits for weights and activations, QAT is performed to help correct errors from model quantization.
elements.sent-2411-132: However, the model may still lack robustness for ACiM applications.
elements.sent-2411-133: To build a model that can withstand ADC quantization and analog noise simultaneously, ASiM supports NAT, enhancing model robustness as described by the equation: \begin{equation}\widetilde{O} = O \cdot (1 + \eta) \label{Eq_NAT}\end{equation} where $O$ is the ideal output activation expected by model inference, and scaled Gaussian noise, $\eta \sim \mathcal{N (0, \sigma^2)$, is applied during the forward pass to emulate the noisy outputs generated by ACiM circuits.}
elements.sent-2411-134: By fine-tuning the DNN model with various noise intensities $\sigma$, we select a model that maintains acceptable baseline accuracy while maximizing noise tolerance for ACiM applications.
elements.sent-2411-135: We conducted NAT on ResNet-18 and ViT-B-32 using the CIFAR-10 and ImageNet datasets, respectively, with the baseline model accuracy under different noise intensities shown in Fig. \ref{Fig_NAT .}
elements.sent-2411-136: As observed in many previous studies, ResNet models tend to be easier to fine-tune compared to ViT models, and the ImageNet task is generally more challenging than CIFAR-10.
elements.sent-2411-137: Consequently, we selected models optimized for ACiM applications, with a 2.0\% accuracy drop for the CIFAR-10 task and around a 3.0\% accuracy drop for the ImageNet task.
elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[width=\columnwidth]{Figure/Fig_NAT.pdf} \caption{Noise-aware training: accuracy of 8b/8b baseline models across different noise intensities. ResNet models demonstrate greater robustness compared to ViT, while ImageNet proves more challenging than CIFAR-10.} \label{Fig_NAT}
elements.subsection-2411-007: Re-consider CSNR with Actual Data Distribution
elements.sent-2411-138: To establish a foundation for evaluating how different design factors impact inference accuracy, we first analyzed the CSNR under realistic neural network data distributions.
elements.sent-2411-139: Our analysis focuses on ResNet-18 and ViT-B-32 architectures using the CIFAR-10 dataset, with both models configured for 6-bit weight and 6-bit activation quantization.
elements.sent-2411-140: The distributions of several common layers processed by ACiM are shown in Fig. \ref{Fig_Input_Dist (a), with their bit-level sparsity for each bit index displayed in Fig. \ref{Fig_Input_Dist (b).}}
elements.sent-2411-141: In convolutional layers, we observed distinct patterns between weights and activations:.
elements.sent-2411-142: Activations show a characteristic of one-sided distribution due to ReLU operations that zero out negative values.
elements.sent-2411-143: Activation bit-level sparsity remains consistently below 40\%, with MSBs being particularly sparse.
elements.sent-2411-144: Weights exhibit a normal distribution, resulting in approximately 50\% bit-level sparsity across all bit positions.
elements.sent-2411-145: The ViT architecture presents more complex distribution patterns and distinctive sparsity distribution.
elements.sent-2411-147: QKV projection layers show significant cross-channel variations due to LayerNorm operations~\cite{Challenges_Transformer_Quantization .}
elements.sent-2411-148: Both weights and activations display two-sided distributions with numerous small values quantized to zero in per-tensor quantization configuration.
elements.sent-2411-149: By these reasons, bit-level sparsity in QKV projections consistently ranges between 20\% and 30\% for both weights and activations.
elements.sent-2411-150: The self-attention computation demonstrates particularly diverse characteristics:.
elements.sent-2411-151: Value (V) matrices follow a normal distribution with approximately 50\% bit-level sparsity.
elements.sent-2411-152: In most cases, attention scores (A) exhibit a long-tail distribution characterized by predominantly small values with occasional large outliers, resulting in bit-level sparsity varies dramatically from 0\% to 60\%, with MSBs showing exceptionally high sparsity.
elements.figure-2411-004: \subfloat[]{ \includegraphics[width=0.98\linewidth]{Figure/Fig_Input_Dist.pdf} \label{Fig_Input_Dist_a} } \subfloat[]{ \includegraphics[width=0.98\linewidth]{Figure/Fig_Input_Sparsity.pdf} \label{Fig_Input_Dist_b} } \caption{Quantized DNN model input distribution analysis. (a) Data distribution of representative DNN layers after 6b/6b quantization. elements.sent-2411-162: We begin our exploration of inference accuracy by examining the quantization noise introduced by ADCs in ACiM circuits.
elements.sent-2411-163: Unlike DCiM, where inference accuracy is influenced solely by model quantization, the ADC rounding effect in ACiM circuits introduces additional quantization errors to each MAC output.. Boundary Precision.
elements.sent-2411-164: We define ADC boundary precision as the precision that corresponds exactly to the row-parallelism of the ACiM.
elements.sent-2411-165: For example, in a conventional bit-serial ACiM with a row-parallelism dimension of $2^k$, the boundary precision is $k$, which would ideally translate the CBL voltage into digital code.
elements.sent-2411-166: Using an ADC with precision lower than $k$ introduces quantization errors that could degrade inference accuracy, while ADC precision higher than $k$ is redundant and adds unnecessary area and energy overhead.
elements.sent-2411-168: While increasing row-parallelism dimension improves ACiM throughput, it also requires higher ADC precision to maintain the same computational accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-169: In this paper, we selected a row parallelism of 256 as it provides an optimal balance between throughput and tunable ADC precision.
elements.sent-2411-170: An optimized design carefully selects ADC precision slightly below the boundary precision, balancing minor accuracy loss with significant efficiency gains.. Experiments.

elements.sent-2411-252: We presented ASiM in this paper, an open-source simulation framework specifically developed to assess and optimize inference accuracy in SRAM-based ACiM circuits.
elements.sent-2411-253: ASiM allows researchers to easily integrate their ACiM circuits into the PyTorch ecosystem, enabling the evaluation of various design factors under realistic conditions that include quantization noise, analog imperfections, and ADC constraints.
elements.sent-2411-254: Our extensive analysis using ASiM revealed critical insights for ACiM design.
elements.sent-2411-255: Although the bit-parallel approach can substantially improve energy efficiency while tolerating quantization noise, analog noise poses a serious threat to inference accuracy, creating stringent design requirements.
elements.sent-2411-256: We also showed that higher ADC precision is crucial for more complex models and tasks, such as ImageNet and ViT, while CNNs on simpler datasets can reduce ADC precision without significant accuracy loss.
elements.sent-2411-257: Furthermore, we found that largest computation errors primarily occur in the MSB cycles, where even a minor ADC readout error as small as 1 LSB can result in severe inference degradation.
elements.sent-2411-258: To tackle these challenges, we recommended two strategies: HCiM and majority voting, both of which aim to ensure accurate MSB cycle computation.
elements.sent-2411-259: Incorporating these methods shows promise in enhancing inference accuracy without diminishing inherent advantage on energy efficiency, presenting a promising strategy for deploying ACiM reliably in real-world applications.. \bibliographystyle{IEEEtran .}

elements.algorithm-2411-002: \caption{Bit-wise Simulation of ASiM Modules} \label{Algorithm_ASiM} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \REQUIRE Input activations \ENSURE Output activations \STATE Initialize ASiM module with the following parameters: bit width for weights and activations, ADC precision, row parallelism, analog noise intensity, and others \STATE Quantize input activations and weights to $X$ and $W$ bits. Convert them into binary bit tensors and split them into $N$ tensors to match the dimensions of the macros \FOR{Each mapping update: $n = 0, 1, \dots, N-1$} \FOR{Each weight bit index: $i = 0, 1, \dots, W-1$} \FOR{Each input bit index: $j = 0, 1, \dots, X-1$} \STATE Perform bit-wise computation: $V_{MAC} = $nn.functional$(w_i, x_j)$ \IF{Analog noise is included} \STATE Generate and add noise: $V_{MAC} = V_{MAC} + f_{N}(\sigma)$ \ENDIF \STATE Apply ADC rounding function: $MAC_{i,j} = f_{ADC}(V_{MAC})$ \STATE Update multi-bit output activations: $\text{Output} = \text{Output} + \text{Shift}(MAC_{i,j}, i+j)$ \ENDFOR \ENDFOR \ENDFOR \RETURN Output activations computed by the ACiM circuits \end{algorithmic}

elements.sent-2411-365: This paper presents ASiM, a simulation framework for evaluating inference accuracy in SRAM-based ACiM systems.
elements.sent-2411-366: ASiM captures critical effects in SRAM based analog compute in memory systems, such as ADC quantization, bit parallel encoding, and analog noise, which must be modeled with high fidelity due to their distinct behavior in charge domain architectures compared to other memory technologies.
elements.sent-2411-367: ASiM supports a wide range of modern DNN workloads, including CNN and Transformer-based models such as ViT, and scales to large-scale tasks like ImageNet classification.
elements.sent-2411-368: Our results indicate that bit-parallel encoding can improve energy efficiency with only modest accuracy degradation; however, even 1 LSB of analog noise can significantly impair inference performance, particularly in complex tasks such as ImageNet.
elements.sent-2411-369: To address this, we explore hybrid analog–digital execution and majority voting schemes, both of which enhance robustness without negating energy savings.
elements.sent-2411-370: ASiM bridges the gap between hardware design and inference performance, offering actionable insights for energy-efficient, high-accuracy ACiM deployment.
elements.sent-2411-371: Compute-in-Memory (CiM) has emerged as a novel paradigm to overcome these limitations by performing computations directly within the memory arrays ~\cite{IMC_Advances_Prospects .}
elements.sent-2411-372: By reducing data movement between logic and memory, CiM architectures can achieve significant improvements in both energy efficiency and throughput.
elements.sent-2411-373: Among CiM architectures, analog compute-in-memory (ACiM) offers high computational density and energy efficiency by leveraging the analog properties of memory cells to perform multiply-and-accumulate (MAC) operations directly in the analog domain~\cite{Analog_or_Digital .}
elements.sent-2411-374: SRAM-based ACiM, in particular, has attracted attention due to its compatibility with standard CMOS processes and reliable charge-domain operation~\cite{64-Tile .}
elements.sent-2411-375: These techniques, while beneficial in some respects, exacerbate quantization errors and make it difficult to preserve inference accuracy, highlighting the importance of accurate and thorough evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-376: We examined several existing simulation frameworks aimed at assessing inference accuracy and observed significant variation in their predictions.
elements.sent-2411-377: These discrepancies suggest that current tools may struggle to provide consistent guidance for design validation or silicon correlation.
elements.sent-2411-378: A key contributing factor is the lack of standardized, open-source simulators tailored specifically to SRAM-based ACiM.
elements.sent-2411-379: The inconsistent predictions produced by different tools highlight a key research question: What modeling components are essential for accurate inference evaluation in SRAM-based ACiM systems, and how can we ensure reliable, consistent predictions across diverse design scenarios? Addressing this gap would support the development of standardized evaluation practices and facilitate the broader adoption of ACiM architectures.
elements.sent-2411-380: In this paper, we introduce ASiM, an accurate simulation framework that bridges low-level circuit behavior and high-level inference performance in SRAM-based ACiM systems.
elements.sent-2411-381: ASiM models essential circuit effects such as ADC quantization, bit-parallel encoding, and analog noise, enabling accurate evaluation of how hardware design choices impact DNN inference accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-382: Designed for practical use, ASiM integrates seamlessly with PyTorch as a plug-and-play tool, providing both modeling fidelity and ease of deployment.
elements.sent-2411-383: It supports SRAM ACiM specific architectural innovations, including bit-parallel execution, and is compatible with modern DNNs such as Transformers.
elements.sent-2411-384: Using ASiM, we conduct a detailed analysis of how various circuit-level factors influence inference accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-385: Analog noise within ACiM circuits severely impact inference accuracy: Our analysis reveals that even minor ADC readout errors of 1 LSB can propagate through the network and cause significant accuracy degradation.
elements.sent-2411-386: Complex tasks demand stricter hardware design constraints: Inference on tasks like ImageNet is substantially more sensitive to circuit-level nonidealities than simpler benchmarks such as CIFAR-10.
elements.sent-2411-387: Maintaining accuracy on such tasks requires tighter design margins or the adoption of more robust approaches like Hybrid CiM (HCiM).
elements.sent-2411-388: Bit-parallel ACiM improves energy efficiency but increases sensitivity to noise: While this architecture tolerates moderate quantization noise and achieves substantial energy savings, it is more susceptible to analog noise, necessitating stricter design standards to ensure inference reliability.
elements.sent-2411-389: We develop ASiM, an open-source simulation framework for systematic inference accuracy evaluation in SRAM-based ACiM systems.
elements.sent-2411-390: ASiM integrates with PyTorch and supports modern DNN architectures, including Transformers.
elements.sent-2411-391: With ASiM, we analyze the impact of key circuit-level design factors on inference accuracy and uncover how specific noise sources degrade model performance, revealing sensitivity issues often overlooked in prior work.
elements.sent-2411-392: Building on our findings, we provide practical design guidelines and solutions to improve ACiM inference accuracy, enhancing its practicality for real-world applications.

elements.sent-2411-415: ASiM is implemented within PyTorch to ensure seamless integration with modern DNN workflows.
elements.sent-2411-416: It provides a plug-and-play simulation framework tailored for SRAM-based charge-domain ACiM.
elements.sent-2411-417: For MAC-heavy operations such as CONV, Linear, and Attention, ASiM modules can replace standard PyTorch layers, enabling bit-wise simulations with user-defined circuit parameters.
elements.sent-2411-418: During inference, quantized weights and activations are decomposed into binary bit tensors, split according to the predefined macro dimensions, and mapped onto the ACiM macros.
elements.sent-2411-419: The bit-wise simulation transforms the original floating-point operations into $\text{wbit \times \text{xbit \times \text{mapping}$ operation is simulated using native PyTorch functions, producing ideal analog voltages on CBLs.}}
elements.sent-2411-420: ASiM then applies analog noise and ADC effects to these voltages.
elements.sent-2411-421: By leveraging PyTorch’s CUDA acceleration, ASiM achieves both simulation fidelity and high performance.
elements.sent-2411-422: Additionally, ASiM supports bit-parallel operation, where input activations are encoded based on encoding bit width, and the framework calculates bit-parallel voltage values using pre-configured algorithms, ensuring compatibility with most latest ACiM designs.
elements.sent-2411-423: As ASiM is designed as a system-level simulator, it does not model or differentiate between specific low-level analog noise sources.
elements.sent-2411-424: Instead, the cumulative effect of these noise sources is abstracted as equivalent fluctuations on CBL voltage for ADC readout.
elements.sent-2411-425: Therefore, ASiM includes two simple noise models: random noise and nonlinearity, both of which can be configured with user-defined noise intensities.
elements.sent-2411-426: Nonlinearity arises from device mismatches and layout-specific issues; ASiM incorporates capacitance mismatch in local memory cells and DACs, scaled by $\sqrt{N}$, where $N$ is the number of capacitors in the CBL and DAC~\cite{Cap_Non_linear .}
elements.sent-2411-427: Due to the stochastic nature of mapping DNNs to ACiM hardware, spatial noise such as mismatch and PVT variation, is expected to have a similar impact on accuracy and is thus integrated into the overall random noise and nonlinearity model in this paper.
elements.sent-2411-428: Users can also incorporate custom noise models to account for nonlinearities specific to their designs, enabling tailored inference performance analysis with ASiM.

elements.sent-2411-438: Although each framework has its strengths, these modeling differences can lead to diverging accuracy predictions.

elements.sent-2411-442: To build a model that can withstand ADC quantization and analog noise simultaneously, ASiM supports NAT, enhancing model robustness as described by the equation: \begin{equation}\widetilde{O} = O \cdot (1 + \eta), \label{Eq_NAT}\end{equation} where $O$ is the ideal output activation expected by model inference, and scaled Gaussian noise, $\eta \sim \mathcal{N (0, \sigma^2)$, is applied directly during the floating-point forward pass to emulate the noisy outputs generated by ACiM circuits.}

elements.sent-2411-476: In particular, the MAC output of each ACiM column is proportional to the bit-level sparsity of weights and activations.
elements.sent-2411-477: The expected MAC value at an ACiM column can be expressed as: \begin{equation}P(DP = 1) = P(w = 1 \cap x = 1) = P(w = 1) \cdot P(x = 1), \label{Eq_DP_Probability}\end{equation} \begin{equation}E(MAC) = n \cdot P(DP = 1). \label{Eq_MAC}\end{equation} Here, the expected MAC output equals the product of the row parallelism $n$ and the probability that a local dot product evaluates to "1".
elements.sent-2411-478: This probability further decomposes into the product of the bit-level sparsity of weights and activations~\cite{PACiM .}
